Eliphaz thought that because Job complained so much about his suffering, he believed God was unjust in afflicting him; but Job did not think so. What Eliphaz says is wrongly applied to Job, but it is true that when God does good for us, it is not because He owes it to us. A person's piety benefits God not at all, nor brings Him any gain. The benefits of religion to people are far greater than the losses from it. God is a Sovereign who gives no account of His actions; but He is perfectly wise, just, faithful, good, and merciful. He approves of the likeness of His own holiness and delights in the fruits of His Spirit; He accepts the thankful service of the humble believer while He rejects the proud claim of the self-confident.

Eliphaz brought serious charges against Job without any reason for his accusations except that Job was visited as he supposed God always visits every wicked person. He accuses him of oppression and says that he did harm with his wealth and power during a time of prosperity.

Eliphaz wanted Job to observe the path that wicked people have taken, and see what the end of their way was. It is good for us to observe it so we do not walk in it. But if others are destroyed and we are spared, instead of blaming them and exalting ourselves, as Eliphaz does here, we should be thankful to God and take it as a warning.

Eliphaz’s answer wrongly implied that Job had previously not known God, and that prosperity in this life would follow his sincere conversion. The advice Eliphaz gives is good, though, for Job it was based on the false idea that he was a stranger and enemy to God. Let us beware of slandering our brothers; and if we are afflicted in this manner, let us remember how Job was treated; yes, how Jesus was reviled, so that we may be patient. Let us examine whether there might be some reason for the slander, and walk carefully so as to avoid all appearances of evil.
